perturbation.original_text: Within forty-five (45) days following the end of each Calendar Quarter after the First Commercial Sale of each Licensed Product in the Territory, Vyera shall provide CytoDyn with a report in a form reasonably acceptable to CytoDyn containing the following information for the applicable Calendar Quarter for such Licensed Product: (a) the amount of gross sales of the Licensed Product in the Territory; (b) an itemized calculation of Net Sales in the Territory showing reasonably detailed deductions; provided for in the definition of "Net Sales"; (c) a reasonably detailed calculation of the royalty payment due on such sales; (d) an accounting of the number of units of the Licensed Product sold; and (e) the application of the reduction, if any, made in accordance with the terms of Section 8.7. Vyera shall pay all amounts due to CytoDyn with respect to Net Sales by Vyera or its Affiliates for such Calendar Quarter at the time of the submission of such quarterly report.
perturbation.changed_text: Within one hundred twenty (120) days following the end of each Calendar Quarter after the First Commercial Sale of each Licensed Product in the Territory, Vyera shall provide CytoDyn with a report in a form reasonably acceptable to CytoDyn containing the following information for the applicable Calendar Quarter for such Licensed Product: (a) the amount of gross sales of the Licensed Product in the Territory; (b) an itemized calculation of Net Sales in the Territory showing reasonably detailed deductions; provided for in the definition of "Net Sales"; (c) a reasonably detailed calculation of the royalty payment due on such sales; (d) an accounting of the number of units of the Licensed Product sold; and (e) the application of the reduction, if any, made in accordance with the terms of Section 8.7. Vyera shall pay all amounts due to CytoDyn with respect to Net Sales by Vyera or its Affiliates for such Calendar Quarter at the time of the submission of such quarterly report.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the report deadline to 120 days introduces a contradiction with standard accounting and reporting practices and may violate SEC regulations regarding timely financial reporting.
perturbation.law_explanation: This regulation mandates the filing of quarterly reports on Form 10-Q within a specific timeframe. While this contract is between private entities, delaying the royalty report to 120 days could impede CytoDyn's ability to accurately and promptly fulfill its own SEC reporting requirements, effectively creating a situation where they are unable to meet their obligation under 17 CFR § 240.13a-13.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.13a-13 - Quarterly reports on Form 10-Q (§ 249.308a of this chapter). | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.13a-13 - Quarterly reports on Form 10-Q (§ 249.308a of this chapter). CFR prev | next § 240.13a-13 Quarterly reports on Form 10-Q (§ 249.308a of this chapter). (a) Except as provided in paragraphs (b) and (c) of this section , every issuer that has securities registered pursuant to section 12 of the Act and is required to file annual reports pursuant to section 13 of the Act, and has filed or intends to file such reports on Form 10-K ( § 249.310 of this chapter), shall file a quarterly report on Form 10-Q (§ 249.308a of this chapter) within the period specified in General Instruction A.1. to that form for each of the first three quarters of each fiscal year of the issuer , commencing with the first fiscal quarter following the most recent fiscal year for which full financial statements were included in the registration statement , or, if the registration statement included financial statements for an interim period subsequent to the most recent fiscal year end meeting the requirements of Article 10 of Regulation S-X and Rule 8-03 of Regulation S-X for smaller reporting companies, for the first fiscal quarter subsequent to the quarter reported upon in the registration statement . The first quarterly report of the issuer shall be filed either within 45 days after the effective date of the registration statement or on or before the date on which such report would have been required to be filed if the issuer has been required to file reports on Form 10-Q as of its last fiscal quarter, whichever is later. (b) The provisions of this rule shall not apply to the following issuers: (1) Investment companies required to file reports pursuant to § 270.30a-1 ; (2) Foreign private issuers required to file reports pursuant to § 240.13a-16 ; and (3) Asset-backed issu
perturbation.justification: The original contract stipulates a report deadline of 45 days, while the changed contract extends it to 120 days. The Law Explanation suggests this delay could impede CytoDyn's ability to meet its SEC reporting requirements, potentially violating 17 CFR § 240.13a-13, which pertains to quarterly reports on Form 10-Q. The scraped law snippet 2, specifically 17 CFR § 240.13a-13 (a), states that every issuer required to file annual reports pursuant to section 13 of the Act, and has filed such reports on Form 10-K shall file a quarterly report on Form 10-Q within the period specified in General Instruction A.1. to that form for each of the first three quarters of each fiscal year of the issuer. While the exact deadline isn't specified in the snippets provided, the extension to 120 days directly increases the risk of non-compliance, as the delay may push CytoDyn beyond the legally permissible timeframe for filing its own quarterly reports. Thus, the changed text potentially contradicts the timely reporting requirements mandated by federal regulations.

perturbation.original_text: Upon reasonable prior notice, but not more than once per Calendar Year, such records of Vyera and its Affiliates shall be available during Vyera's and its Affiliates regular business hours for a period of three (3) years from the end of the Calendar Year to which they pertain for examination at the expense of CytoDyn by an independent certified public accountant selected by CytoDyn and reasonably acceptable to Vyera, for the sole purpose of verifying the accuracy of the financial reports and correctness of the payments furnished by Vyera pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon reasonable prior notice, but not more than once per Calendar Year, such records of Vyera and its Affiliates shall be available during Vyera's and its Affiliates regular business hours for a period of one (1) year from the end of the Calendar Year to which they pertain for examination at the expense of CytoDyn by an independent certified public accountant selected by CytoDyn and reasonably acceptable to Vyera, for the sole purpose of verifying the accuracy of the financial reports and correctness of the payments furnished by Vyera pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the record availability period to one year contradicts standard accounting practices and the IRS's record-keeping requirements for audit purposes, which is generally three years.
perturbation.law_explanation: 26 U.S. Code § 6501 establishes a three-year statute of limitations for assessing tax liabilities. By limiting the availability of records to one year, the agreement hinders CytoDyn's ability to verify royalty payments and potentially discover underpayments within the legally prescribed timeframe for tax-related audits and adjustments. This creates a contractual impediment to exercising rights provided under federal law.

Limitations on assessment and collection (a) General rule Except as otherwise provided in this section, the amount of any tax imposed by this title shall be assessed within 3 years after the return was filed (whether or not such return was filed on or after the date prescribed) or, if the tax is payable by stamp, at any time aft
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 26 U.S. Code § 6501 - Limitations on assessment and collection | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 26 U.S. Code § 6501 - Limitations on assessment and collection U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) General rule Except as otherwise provided in this section, the amount of any tax imposed by this title shall be assessed within 3 years after the return was filed (whether or not such return was filed on or after the date prescribed) or, if the tax is payable by stamp, at any time after such tax became due and before the expiration of 3 years after the date on which any part of such tax was paid, and no proceeding in court without assessment for the collection of such tax shall be begun after the expiration of such period. For purposes of this chapter, the term “ return ” means the return required to be filed by the taxpayer (and does not include a return of any person from whom the taxpayer has received an item of income, gain, loss, deduction, or credit). (b) Time return deemed filed (1) Early return For purposes of this section, a return of tax imposed by this title, except tax imposed by chapter 3, 4, 21, or 24, filed before the last day prescribed by law or by regulations promulgated pursuant to law for the filing thereof, shall be considered as filed on such last day. (2) Return of certain employment and withholding taxes For purposes of this section, if a return of tax imposed by chapter 3, 4, 21, or 24 for any period ending with or within a calendar year is filed before April 15 of the succeeding calendar year, such return shall be considered filed on April 15 of such calendar year. (3) Return executed by Secretary Notwithstanding the provisions of paragraph (2) of section 6020(b), the execution of a return by the Secretary pursuant to the authority conferred by such section shall not start the running of the period of limitations on assessment and collectio
perturbation.justification: The modified contract, by reducing the record availability period to one year, directly contradicts the standard three-year statute of limitations for assessing tax liabilities as established in 26 U.S. Code § 6501. This is supported by the law snippets provided, which state that the amount of any tax imposed by this title shall be assessed within 3 years after the return was filed. By limiting access to records to only one year, the agreement impedes CytoDyn's ability to verify royalty payments and potentially discover underpayments within the legally prescribed timeframe for tax-related audits. Therefore, the modified text introduces a clear non-compliance, making the terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees that, during the Term and for a period of ten (10) years thereafter, a Party (the "Receiving Party") receiving Confidential Information of the other Party (the "Disclosing Party") shall: (a) maintain in confidence such Confidential Information using not less than the efforts such Receiving Party uses to maintain in confidence its own confidential or proprietary information of similar kind and value; (b) not disclose such Confidential Information to any Third Party without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, except for disclosures expressly permitted below; and (c) not use such Confidential Information for any purpose except those permitted by this Agreement (it being understood that this Section 10.1 shall not create or imply any rights or licenses not expressly granted under this Agreement). Notwithstanding anything to the contrary in this Agreement, the obligations of confidentiality and non-use with respect to any Know-How or trade secret within such Confidential Information shall survive such ten (10) year period for so long as such Confidential Information remains Know-How and/or protected as a trade secret under Applicable Law.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees that, during the Term and for a period of two (2) years thereafter, a Party (the "Receiving Party") receiving Confidential Information of the other Party (the "Disclosing Party") shall: (a) maintain in confidence such Confidential Information using not less than the efforts such Receiving Party uses to maintain in confidence its own confidential or proprietary information of similar kind and value; (b) not disclose such Confidential Information to any Third Party without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, except for disclosures expressly permitted below; and (c) not use such Confidential Information for any purpose except those permitted by this Agreement (it being understood that this Section 10.1 shall not create or imply any rights or licenses not expressly granted under this Agreement). Notwithstanding anything to the contrary in this Agreement, the obligations of confidentiality and non-use with respect to any Know-How or trade secret within such Confidential Information shall survive such two (2) year period for so long as such Confidential Information remains Know-How and/or protected as a trade secret under Applicable Law.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to two years may not adequately protect trade secrets, which, under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), require reasonable measures to maintain confidentiality. Two years may be deemed insufficient for certain long-term trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S. Code § 1832 defines trade secret theft. For information to qualify for trade secret protection under federal law, reasonable measures must be taken to protect its secrecy. A two-year confidentiality period may be deemed insufficient to constitute 'reasonable measures' for certain types of trade secrets, particularly in the pharmaceutical industry where development cycles and market exclusivity periods are longer. This short duration potentially weakens the protection afforded to trade secrets, increasing vulnerability to misappropriation and undermining the 'reasonable measures' requirement for trade secret status.

Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from ten years to two years. The Law Explanation states that a two-year confidentiality period may be insufficient to constitute 'reasonable measures' for certain types of trade secrets, potentially weakening the protection afforded to trade secrets. Although the snippets do not explicitly state a required timeframe, the Law Explanation, combined with the Perturbation Explanation, suggests that this reduction may undermine the 'reasonable measures' requirement necessary for trade secret protection under 18 U.S. Code § 1832, as maintaining confidentiality is key to the protection of trade secrets.
